docs.title: Prostate Cancer Research Library
docs.text:
This is a curated, patient-perspective research interface for prostate cancer diagnosis and treatment decisions.

Not medical advice

This repository summarizes research for education and clinician discussion. It does not diagnose, prescribe, recommend a treatment, or replace medical care.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The repository is designed to help a potential patient understand:

  • What tests and diagnostic steps usually mean
  • How clinicians classify risk and stage disease
  • Treatment options and trade-offs
  • Side effects and quality-of-life outcomes
  • When active surveillance may be considered
  • How new evidence may change questions to ask clinicians

docs.title: Default evidence hierarchy
docs.text:
1. Current clinical guidelines and consensus statements from reputable oncology/urology bodies
  2. Randomized controlled trials and high-quality meta-analyses
  3. Large prospective cohorts and registry studies
  4. Diagnostic accuracy studies with clinically relevant outcomes
  5. Patient-reported outcome studies
  6. Mechanistic, preclinical, or hypothesis-generating studies, clearly labeled

docs.title: Core patient questions
docs.text:
• Is cancer present, and how certain is that conclusion?
  • If present, how aggressive does it appear to be?
  • What are the reasonable management options for this risk group?
  • What are the likely benefits, harms, uncertainties, and quality-of-life effects?
  • What would change the decision later?

docs.title: Category Index
docs.text:
Use these categories to file studies, guideline updates, patient decision aids, and clinical questions.

Category What belongs here Patient-facing question Screening & Early Detection PSA, MRI-first pathways, risk calculators, screening intervals, overdiagnosis Should I be tested, retested, or monitored? Diagnostic Workup Biopsy approaches, MRI interpretation, pathology, infection/bleeding risk How reliable is the diagnosis? Risk Stratification & Staging Gleason/Grade Group, PSA density, TNM, nomograms, genomic classifiers How aggressive is it? Active Surveillance Eligibility, monitoring schedules, triggers for treatment, long-term outcomes Can treatment safely wait? Localized Treatment Surgery, radiation modalities, focal therapy, comparative outcomes What are the curative options and trade-offs? Advanced & Recurrent Disease ADT, AR pathway inhibitors, chemotherapy, radioligand therapy, recurrence What if it has spread or returns? Imaging & Biomarkers mpMRI, PSMA PET, liquid/tissue biomarkers, genomic tests What extra tests might clarify risk? Side Effects & Quality of Life Urinary, sexual, bowel, fatigue, mental health, survivorship What will life be like after treatment? Nutrition Diet quality, body composition, metabolic health, supplements, nutrition during treatment and survivorship What nutrition evidence is relevant, and what should I discuss with a clinician or dietitian? Exercise Resistance training, aerobic fitness, fatigue, function, bone/muscle health, exercise during treatment and survivorship What activity is safe and useful in my situation? Shared Decision-Making Decision aids, patient preferences, communication, second opinions How do I choose between reasonable options? Guidelines & Care Pathways NCCN/EAU/AUA/ASCO/NICE updates, multidisciplinary pathways What do authoritative guidelines say?

docs.title: Filing metadata
docs.text:
Each filed item should include:

  • Citation and source URL/DOI/PMID
  • Publication date and retrieval date
  • Evidence type and population
  • Category or categories
  • Patient relevance
  • Main finding
  • Limitations and uncertainty
  • Clinical questions raised

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Research notes and filed articles about exercise, physical function, resistance training, aerobic fitness, fatigue, treatment tolerance, and quality of life in prostate cancer diagnosis, treatment, and survivorship.

This category is for evidence that helps patients discuss safe, appropriate activity plans with clinicians and exercise professionals.

docs.title: Key patient questions
docs.text:
• What kinds of exercise improve fatigue, function, strength, mood, or quality of life during or after prostate cancer treatment?
  • What is known about exercise during active surveillance, after prostatectomy, during radiation, or during ADT?
  • Can resistance training help reduce muscle and bone loss associated with ADT?
  • What safety limits apply after surgery, during radiation, or with bone metastases?
  • What should be discussed with a clinician, physiotherapist, or accredited exercise physiologist?

docs.title: Evidence to prioritize
docs.text:
• Randomized trials and meta-analyses of exercise interventions in prostate cancer populations
  • Studies measuring fatigue, strength, cardiorespiratory fitness, falls, bone density, sexual/urinary function, mood, and quality of life
  • Exercise oncology guidelines and consensus statements
  • Safety studies for patients on ADT or with advanced/metastatic disease

docs.title: Cautions
docs.text:
Not medical advice

Exercise notes are educational. People undergoing diagnosis or treatment should discuss exercise plans with a qualified clinician, especially after surgery, during systemic therapy, or if bone metastases, severe fatigue, anemia, cardiovascular disease, falls risk, or pain are present.

Be especially cautious with:

  • Heavy lifting soon after surgery
  • High-impact exercise with bone metastases or fracture risk
  • Intense exercise during acute illness, severe fatigue, or treatment complications
  • Generic fitness advice not studied in prostate cancer populations

docs.title: Filed items
docs.text: • Exercise and Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map — curated evidence summary covering exercise/physical activity before diagnosis, after diagnosis, during treatment, and after treatment/survivorship. Includes incidence evidence, post-diagnosis survival/progression cohorts, radiation/ADT trials, pelvic-floor rehabilitation evidence, and exercise-oncology guidance.

docs.title: Current evidence summary
docs.text: Patient phase Summary Before diagnosis / incidence Exercise is highly beneficial for general health, but prostate-cancer-specific incidence prevention evidence is mixed. Best meta-analyses show little or no association with total prostate cancer incidence, with possible but uncertain reductions in advanced/aggressive/fatal disease. After diagnosis Higher post-diagnosis activity, especially brisk walking and vigorous activity, is consistently associated with better survival/progression outcomes in observational cohorts, but causality is not proven. During treatment Randomized trials and systematic reviews support aerobic and resistance exercise during radiation and ADT for fatigue, fitness, strength, function, body composition, and quality of life. After treatment Survivorship exercise supports function and quality of life. Pelvic-floor muscle exercise, especially supervised programs, has specific evidence for urinary incontinence after radical prostatectomy.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Research notes and filed articles about nutrition, diet quality, body composition, metabolic health, and prostate cancer diagnosis/treatment outcomes.

This category is for patient-facing evidence on nutrition as a supportive health factor, not as a substitute for clinically indicated cancer diagnosis or treatment.

docs.title: Key patient questions
docs.text:
• What dietary patterns are associated with prostate cancer risk, progression, recurrence, or treatment tolerance?
  • What is known about body weight, body composition, insulin resistance, cardiometabolic health, and outcomes?
  • Are there nutrition considerations during active surveillance, surgery, radiation, ADT, chemotherapy, or survivorship?
  • Which claims are supported by human clinical evidence, and which remain speculative?
  • What should be discussed with an oncologist, urologist, dietitian, or GP?

docs.title: Evidence to prioritize
docs.text:
• Human randomized trials and high-quality meta-analyses
  • Large prospective cohort studies with clinically meaningful endpoints
  • Studies of diet quality, Mediterranean-style patterns, protein adequacy, weight change, metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and cardiovascular risk in prostate cancer populations
  • Patient-reported outcomes and treatment-tolerance studies
  • Nutrition guidance from reputable cancer organizations

docs.title: Cautions
docs.text:
Not medical advice

Nutrition notes are educational and should not be used to delay diagnosis, avoid treatment, or replace advice from a qualified clinician.

Be especially cautious with:

  • High-dose supplements or concentrated extracts
  • Claims that a diet can cure cancer
  • Major weight-loss or fasting programs during active treatment
  • Interactions between supplements and surgery, radiation, ADT, chemotherapy, anticoagulants, or other medications

docs.title: Paper Repository
docs.text: This section can hold deeper notes on individual papers or curated paper collections.

docs.title: Curated collections
docs.text: • Exercise and Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map — best available evidence on exercise/physical activity before diagnosis, after diagnosis, during treatment, and after treatment/survivorship.

docs.title: Paper note template
docs.text:
# Title

- Citation:
- DOI/PMID:
- URL:
- Date:
- Category:
- Evidence type:
- Population:
- Patient relevance:
- Main finding:
- Limitations:
- Questions for clinician:

docs.title: Exercise and Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map
docs.text:
Retrieved: 2026-07-04 Category: Exercise Scope: exercise/physical activity before diagnosis, after diagnosis, during treatment, and after treatment.

Not medical advice

This page summarizes evidence for education and clinician discussion. It does not recommend any exercise program, cancer screening decision, or treatment choice. Exercise plans should be discussed with qualified clinicians, especially after surgery, during radiation or systemic therapy, with cardiovascular disease, severe fatigue, anemia, pain, falls risk, or possible bone metastases.

docs.title: Patient-facing bottom line
docs.text: Phase Best-supported conclusion Strength of evidence Practical question for clinician Before diagnosis / incidence Physical activity is clearly beneficial for general health, but prostate-cancer-specific incidence findings are mixed. Meta-analyses suggest little or no association with total prostate cancer incidence; some studies suggest lower risk of advanced/aggressive or fatal disease. Moderate for "mixed/uncertain total incidence"; lower-to-moderate for advanced/fatal subtypes Does my overall health profile make exercise especially important regardless of uncertain prostate-cancer incidence effects? After diagnosis Observational cohorts consistently associate higher post-diagnosis activity, especially brisk walking/vigorous activity, with lower all-cause mortality and possibly lower prostate-cancer-specific mortality/progression. Moderate observational evidence; not proof of causation What activity level is safe for my risk group and treatment plan? During active treatment Randomized trials and reviews support aerobic and resistance exercise for fatigue, physical fitness, strength, body composition, and quality of life during radiation and ADT. These trials generally evaluate symptoms/function rather than cancer control. Moderate interventional evidence for function/QoL; limited for survival/cancer-control endpoints Should I be referred to physiotherapy or an exercise physiologist during treatment? After treatment / survivorship Exercise rehabilitation and pelvic-floor muscle training can help selected post-treatment problems, especially urinary incontinence after prostatectomy and fatigue/function after treatment. Moderate for pelvic-floor training; moderate/variable for broader rehabilitation outcomes What post-treatment exercise or pelvic-floor program is appropriate, supervised, and timed safely?

docs.title: Exercise types covered
docs.text:
• Walking / brisk walking: most clinically approachable activity in cohort evidence after diagnosis.
  • Vigorous activity: associated with better survival/progression outcomes in some cohorts, but subject to healthy-user and reverse-causation issues.
  • Aerobic training: RCT evidence during radiation/ADT supports fitness and fatigue/QoL outcomes.
  • Resistance training: RCT evidence supports muscle mass, strength, and function, especially relevant during ADT.
  • Combined aerobic + resistance programs: common in ADT/survivorship trials and exercise oncology guidance.
  • Pelvic-floor muscle exercise: specific post-prostatectomy urinary incontinence evidence; supervised programs appear more effective than unsupervised.

docs.title: 1. Before diagnosis: incidence and risk of developing prostate cancer
docs.text:
The most prostate-specific systematic reviews do not support a simple claim that exercise prevents all prostate cancer. Benke et al. (2018) found the highest vs lowest overall physical activity had a summary relative risk close to null for total prostate cancer incidence, while estimates for advanced disease trended lower but were not definitive. Liu et al. (2011) found a modest inverse association when combining observational studies, but results varied by design and measurement.

Large general-population cohort syntheses (Moore et al. 2016; Garcia et al. 2023) show strong benefits of physical activity for many chronic diseases and several cancers, but prostate cancer is not among the most consistently reduced cancer sites. Orsini et al. (2009) is a useful cohort suggesting lifetime activity, occupational activity, and walking/bicycling may be associated with lower prostate cancer incidence and mortality, but observational confounding remains important.

Interpretation: exercise should be viewed as strongly beneficial for overall health and treatment resilience, but not as a reliable prostate-cancer prevention strategy.

docs.title: 2. After diagnosis: progression, prostate-cancer mortality, and overall survival
docs.text:
The most directly relevant patient evidence is observational. Kenfield et al. (2011), Richman et al. (2011), Friedenreich et al. (2016), Wang et al. (2017), and the 2026 post-diagnosis meta-analysis all point in the same broad direction: men who are more physically active after diagnosis tend to have better overall survival, and in several analyses lower prostate-cancer-specific mortality or progression.

Key caution: these are not exercise-prescription trials powered for mortality. Men who feel well enough to exercise may differ in disease severity, comorbidity, treatment response, and health behaviors. Still, the consistency makes physical activity a reasonable topic for clinician discussion after diagnosis.

docs.title: 3. During active treatment: radiation and ADT
docs.text:
RCTs and systematic reviews support exercise as supportive care. Segal et al. (2009) tested resistance or aerobic exercise during radiation. Horgan and O'Donovan (2018) summarized RCTs during radiotherapy. For ADT, resistance and combined programs are especially relevant because ADT can worsen body composition, muscle, strength, metabolic risk, fatigue, and bone health. Houben et al. (2023), Alibhai et al. (2024), and Toohey et al. (2022) support feasibility and functional benefits of exercise programs in men on ADT.

Interpretation: exercise during treatment is best framed as symptom/function/risk-factor support, not as a proven anti-cancer treatment.

docs.title: 4. After treatment has ended: recovery and survivorship
docs.text:
Post-treatment evidence is strongest for specific functional outcomes. Pelvic-floor muscle exercise is relevant after radical prostatectomy; Baumann et al. (2022) found supervised pelvic-floor muscle exercise more effective than unsupervised exercise for urinary incontinence recovery. Broader exercise oncology guidance (Campbell et al. 2019) supports exercise to improve fatigue, physical function, and quality of life across cancer survivorship, with tailoring to cancer type, treatment effects, and safety risks.

Interpretation: after treatment, exercise is mainly a recovery, function, quality-of-life, and long-term health strategy. Evidence for recurrence/survival benefit is promising but mostly observational.

docs.title: Physical activity in relation to risk of prostate cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Benke IN, Leitzmann MF, Behrens G, Schmid D. Annals of Oncology. 2018.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1093/annonc/mdy073; PMID 29788165
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29788165/
  • Date: 2018 May 1
  • Category: Exercise; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.
  • Population: 48 cohort and 24 case-control studies; 151,748 prostate cancer cases.
  • Patient relevance: Best prostate-specific synthesis for whether physical activity affects prostate cancer incidence.
  • Main finding: Overall physical activity was close to null for total prostate cancer incidence; estimates for advanced disease trended lower but were not definitive.
  • Limitations: Observational evidence, heterogeneous activity measures, detection/screening bias, and residual confounding.
  • Questions for clinician: Should exercise be emphasized for general health and treatment resilience rather than as prostate cancer prevention?

docs.title: Does physical activity reduce the risk of prostate cancer? A systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Liu Y, Hu F, Li D, Wang F, Zhu L, Chen W. European Urology. 2011.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.eururo.2011.07.007; PMID 21802197
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21802197/
  • Date: 2011 Nov
  • Category: Exercise; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of cohort and case-control studies.
  • Population: 19 cohort and 24 case-control studies; 88,294 prostate cancer cases.
  • Patient relevance: Earlier broad synthesis suggesting possible modest risk reduction.
  • Main finding: Combined observational data suggested total physical activity was associated with lower prostate cancer risk, but findings were inconsistent.
  • Limitations: Older evidence base, variable definitions of activity, observational design, possible publication and detection biases.
  • Questions for clinician: How should older positive evidence be balanced against newer meta-analyses showing weaker prostate-specific incidence associations?

docs.title: Non-occupational physical activity and risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer and mortality outcomes
docs.text:
• Citation: Garcia L, Pearce M, Abbas A, et al. British Journal of Sports Medicine. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1136/bjsports-2022-105669; PMID 36854652
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36854652/
  • Date: 2023 Aug
  • Category: Exercise; Nutrition/metabolic health context
  • Evidence type: Dose-response meta-analysis of large prospective studies.
  • Population: 196 articles; 94 cohorts; more than 30 million participants across multiple outcomes.
  • Patient relevance: Places prostate cancer in the broader context of exercise benefits for mortality, cardiovascular disease, and cancer.
  • Main finding: Non-occupational physical activity was associated with lower risks for many major outcomes; prostate-cancer-specific incidence benefit is less certain than for several other cancers.
  • Limitations: General-population evidence, not prostate-cancer-patient-specific; pooled studies vary in measurement and adjustment.
  • Questions for clinician: What level of activity is appropriate for cardiovascular and cancer survivorship health even if prostate cancer incidence effects are uncertain?

docs.title: Association of Leisure-Time Physical Activity With Risk of 26 Types of Cancer in 1.44 Million Adults
docs.text:
• Citation: Moore SC, Lee IM, Weiderpass E, et al. JAMA Internal Medicine. 2016.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1001/jamainternmed.2016.1548; PMID 27183032
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27183032/
  • Date: 2016 Jun 1
  • Category: Exercise; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Pooled prospective cohort analysis.
  • Population: 1.44 million adults from 12 US and European cohorts.
  • Patient relevance: Large-scale context for cancer prevention claims.
  • Main finding: Leisure-time physical activity was associated with lower risk of several cancers; prostate cancer was not one of the clearest protective associations.
  • Limitations: Self-reported activity, general population, screening/detection bias may differ by activity level.
  • Questions for clinician: Should exercise be discussed as broad cancer/cardiometabolic prevention rather than prostate-specific prevention?

docs.title: A prospective study of lifetime physical activity and prostate cancer incidence and mortality
docs.text:
• Citation: Orsini N, Bellocco R, Bottai M, et al. British Journal of Cancer. 2009.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1038/sj.bjc.6605404; PMID 19861965
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19861965/
  • Date: 2009 Dec 1
  • Category: Exercise; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study.
  • Population: 45,887 Swedish men aged 45–79 years.
  • Patient relevance: Useful cohort on lifetime activity, walking/bicycling, occupational activity, incidence, and mortality.
  • Main finding: Higher lifetime total activity was associated with lower prostate cancer incidence; walking/bicycling and occupational activity showed inverse associations.
  • Limitations: Observational design, activity self-report, confounding, older cohort and screening context.
  • Questions for clinician: Does a history of activity change risk discussion, or mainly support general health advice?

docs.title: Physical activity and survival after prostate cancer diagnosis in the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study
docs.text:
• Citation: Kenfield SA, Stampfer MJ, Giovannucci E, Chan JM. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2011.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.2010.31.5226; PMID 21205749
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21205749/
  • Date: 2011 Feb 20
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Active Surveillance
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study after diagnosis.
  • Population: 2,705 men with nonmetastatic prostate cancer in the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study.
  • Patient relevance: Directly addresses activity after diagnosis and survival.
  • Main finding: Higher post-diagnosis physical activity was associated with lower all-cause mortality and lower prostate-cancer mortality; brisk walking and vigorous activity were notable.
  • Limitations: Observational; healthier men may be more active; not a randomized exercise prescription.
  • Questions for clinician: What level and type of walking or vigorous activity is safe after diagnosis and during the chosen management plan?

docs.title: Physical activity after diagnosis and risk of prostate cancer progression: CaPSURE
docs.text:
• Citation: Richman EL, Kenfield SA, Stampfer MJ, et al. Cancer Research. 2011.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1158/0008-5472.CAN-10-3932; PMID 21610110
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21610110/
  • Date: 2011 Jun 1
  • Category: Exercise; Active Surveillance; Risk Stratification & Staging
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort analysis.
  • Population: 1,455 men with clinically localized prostate cancer.
  • Patient relevance: Focuses on progression, an outcome relevant after diagnosis and during surveillance/treatment decision-making.
  • Main finding: Brisk walking after diagnosis was associated with lower risk of prostate cancer progression; walking pace appeared important.
  • Limitations: Observational, relatively few progression events, activity self-report, possible residual confounding.
  • Questions for clinician: Is brisk walking appropriate during active surveillance or after local treatment, and are there safety limitations?

docs.title: Physical Activity and Survival After Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Friedenreich CM, Wang Q, Neilson HK, et al. European Urology. 2016.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.eururo.2015.12.032; PMID 26774959
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26774959/
  • Date: 2016 Oct
  • Category: Exercise; Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study.
  • Population: 830 stage II–IV incident prostate cancer cases in Alberta, Canada, followed up to 17 years.
  • Patient relevance: Evaluates activity type, intensity, timing, survival, and recurrence/progression.
  • Main finding: Higher post-diagnosis activity was associated with better survival outcomes; activity timing and intensity mattered.
  • Limitations: Observational, self-reported activity, heterogeneous stages and treatments.
  • Questions for clinician: How should activity be adapted by stage, treatment, symptoms, and recurrence risk?

docs.title: Recreational Physical Activity in Relation to Prostate Cancer-specific Mortality Among Men with Nonmetastatic Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Wang Y, Jacobs EJ, Gapstur SM, et al. European Urology. 2017.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.eururo.2017.06.037; PMID 28711382
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28711382/
  • Date: 2017 Dec
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study.
  • Population: Men with nonmetastatic prostate cancer in a US prospective cohort.
  • Patient relevance: Examines recreational activity, including walking, before and after diagnosis in relation to prostate-cancer-specific mortality.
  • Main finding: Higher recreational physical activity before and after diagnosis was associated with lower prostate-cancer-specific mortality in some analyses.
  • Limitations: Observational design, self-reported activity, potential reverse causation and confounding.
  • Questions for clinician: Is a walking-based plan a safe baseline while more individualized exercise advice is arranged?

docs.title: Post-diagnosis physical activity in relation to mortality among prostate cancer survivors: a systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Benker P, Hamann A, Leitzmann MF, Stein MJ. Cancer Causes & Control. 2026.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1007/s10552-026-02197-2; PMID 42348026
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42348026/
  • Date: 2026 Jun 25
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis.
  • Population: 10 studies, more than 50,144 participants, 28,044 deaths.
  • Patient relevance: Current synthesis of post-diagnosis activity and mortality in prostate cancer survivors.
  • Main finding: Higher post-diagnosis physical activity was associated with lower all-cause mortality and lower prostate-cancer-specific mortality.
  • Limitations: Based on observational studies; activity measures and timing vary; causality not established.
  • Questions for clinician: What individualized, sustainable exercise target is reasonable after diagnosis?

docs.title: Randomized controlled trial of resistance or aerobic exercise in men receiving radiation therapy for prostate cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Segal RJ, Reid RD, Courneya KS, et al. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2009.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.2007.15.4963; PMID 19064985
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19064985/
  • Date: 2009 Jan 20
  • Category: Exercise; Localized Treatment; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Randomized controlled trial.
  • Population: 121 men receiving radiotherapy for prostate cancer, with or without ADT.
  • Patient relevance: Direct trial of aerobic vs resistance exercise during radiation.
  • Main finding: Exercise improved selected outcomes such as fatigue, fitness, strength, and quality of life measures compared with usual care; resistance and aerobic exercise had different strengths.
  • Limitations: Moderate sample size; endpoints are supportive-care outcomes, not survival or cancer control.
  • Questions for clinician: During radiation, should exercise emphasize aerobic training, resistance training, or both?

docs.title: The Impact of Exercise during Radiation Therapy for Prostate Cancer on Fatigue and Quality of Life
docs.text:
• Citation: Horgan S, O'Donovan A. Journal of Medical Imaging and Radiation Sciences. 2018.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.jmir.2018.02.056; PMID 32074040
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32074040/
  • Date: 2018 Jun
  • Category: Exercise; Localized Treatment; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer undergoing radiation therapy.
  • Patient relevance: Synthesizes exercise effects on radiation-related fatigue and quality of life.
  • Main finding: Exercise during radiation can help fatigue and quality of life, although effects vary by program and study.
  • Limitations: Limited number of trials, heterogeneous exercise prescriptions, supportive-care endpoints.
  • Questions for clinician: What supervised or home exercise plan is safe during radiotherapy?

docs.title: Resistance Exercise Training Increases Muscle Mass and Strength in Prostate Cancer Patients on ADT
docs.text:
• Citation: Houben LHP, Overkamp M, van Kraaij P, et al. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1249/MSS.0000000000003095; PMID 36534950
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36534950/
  • Date: 2023 Apr 1
  • Category: Exercise; Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Randomized exercise trial.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer receiving ADT.
  • Patient relevance: ADT commonly worsens muscle, strength, body composition, and metabolic health.
  • Main finding: Resistance exercise training increased muscle mass and strength in men on ADT; protein supplementation did not appear to be the main driver compared with exercise.
  • Limitations: Functional/supportive endpoints rather than cancer-control endpoints; program supervision and adherence affect applicability.
  • Questions for clinician: Should resistance training be part of an ADT supportive-care plan, and is referral to an exercise professional appropriate?

docs.title: Home-based versus supervised group exercise in men with prostate cancer on ADT
docs.text:
• Citation: Alibhai SMH, Papadopoulos E, Mina DS, et al. Journal of Geriatric Oncology. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.jgo.2023.101646; PMID 37976654
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37976654/
  • Date: 2024 Jan
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Advanced & Recurrent Disease
  • Evidence type: Multicentre randomized controlled trial and economic analysis.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer on ADT.
  • Patient relevance: Compares practical delivery models: home-based vs supervised group exercise.
  • Main finding: Both home and group models were used for aerobic and resistance training; the study informs feasibility, adherence, outcomes, and costs.
  • Limitations: Delivery model and local resources may affect generalizability; not powered for cancer survival endpoints.
  • Questions for clinician: Is supervised group exercise necessary, or could a structured home program be sufficient and safe?

docs.title: Exercise Adherence in Men with Prostate Cancer Undergoing ADT: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Toohey K, Hunter M, Paterson C, Mortazavi R, Singh B. Cancers. 2022.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.3390/cancers14102452; PMID 35626058
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35626058/
  • Date: 2022 May 16
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Advanced & Recurrent Disease
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer undergoing ADT in exercise intervention studies.
  • Patient relevance: Helps assess whether ADT exercise programs are realistic and beneficial.
  • Main finding: Exercise interventions in men on ADT are feasible and can improve physiological outcomes; adherence and patient experience are central.
  • Limitations: Heterogeneity in programs, supervision, adherence measurement, and outcomes.
  • Questions for clinician: What program design would maximize adherence for my circumstances?

docs.title: Clinical exercise interventions in prostate cancer patients — systematic review of randomized controlled trials
docs.text:
• Citation: Baumann FT, Zopf EM, Bloch W. Supportive Care in Cancer. 2012.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1007/s00520-011-1271-0; PMID 21989678
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21989678/
  • Date: 2012 Feb
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review of randomized controlled trials.
  • Population: Prostate cancer patients during treatment and aftercare.
  • Patient relevance: Broad review of exercise interventions addressing common problems such as fatigue, incontinence, function, and quality of life.
  • Main finding: RCTs generally show positive physiological and psychological effects of exercise interventions in prostate cancer care.
  • Limitations: Older review; trials vary widely in intervention type, treatment phase, and outcome measures.
  • Questions for clinician: Which exercise goals matter most now: fatigue, continence, strength, aerobic fitness, mood, or return to activities?

docs.title: Supervised pelvic floor muscle exercise after radical prostatectomy — systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Baumann FT, Reimer N, Gockeln T, et al. Disability and Rehabilitation. 2022.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1080/09638288.2021.1937717; PMID 34550846
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34550846/
  • Date: 2022 Sep
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Localized Treatment
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis.
  • Population: Men after radical prostatectomy.
  • Patient relevance: Directly relevant to post-surgical urinary incontinence recovery.
  • Main finding: Supervised pelvic-floor muscle exercise was more effective than unsupervised exercise for improving urinary incontinence after radical prostatectomy.
  • Limitations: Program details and timing vary; outcomes depend on adherence and baseline severity.
  • Questions for clinician: Should pelvic-floor physiotherapy start before or after surgery, and should it be supervised?

docs.title: Exercise Guidelines for Cancer Survivors: Consensus Statement
docs.text:
• Citation: Campbell KL, Winters-Stone KM, Wiskemann J, et al. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1249/MSS.0000000000002116; PMID 31626055
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31626055/
  • Date: 2019 Nov
  • Category: Exercise; Guidelines & Care Pathways; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: International multidisciplinary consensus statement.
  • Population: Cancer survivors broadly, with relevance to prostate cancer survivorship and treatment side effects.
  • Patient relevance: Provides practical exercise-oncology framing and safety principles after diagnosis and treatment.
  • Main finding: Exercise is generally safe for cancer survivors when appropriately tailored and can improve fatigue, quality of life, physical function, anxiety, and depressive symptoms.
  • Limitations: Not prostate-only; individual contraindications and treatment-specific risks require clinician input.
  • Questions for clinician: Should I be referred to an accredited exercise physiologist or physiotherapist with oncology experience?

docs.title: Clinician discussion checklist
docs.text:
• Which phase am I in: screening, active surveillance, surgery recovery, radiation, ADT/systemic therapy, or survivorship?
  • Are there any safety limits: bone metastases, anemia, infection, cardiac risk, falls risk, pain, severe fatigue, urinary catheter/wound recovery, or treatment complications?
  • Should the plan emphasize walking, aerobic training, resistance training, pelvic-floor therapy, flexibility/balance, or a combination?
  • Would supervised exercise physiology/physiotherapy be safer or more effective than a home program?
  • What outcomes should be tracked: fatigue, strength, continence, sexual function, bowel symptoms, weight/body composition, glucose/lipids, mood, or PSA/treatment milestones?
  • What symptoms should prompt stopping exercise and contacting the clinical team?

docs.title: Repository interpretation
docs.text:
This evidence strengthens the Exercise category by adding a structured distinction between:

  1. Incidence prevention claims — mixed and should be stated cautiously.
  2. Post-diagnosis prognosis associations — promising but mainly observational.
  3. Supportive-care exercise during treatment — stronger randomized evidence for fatigue, fitness, strength, function, and quality of life.
  4. Post-treatment rehabilitation — strongest for targeted problems such as urinary incontinence after prostatectomy and general survivorship function.

docs.title: Medical Safety Policy
docs.text: This repository is educational and research-focused. It must not provide personal medical advice.

docs.title: Required safety language
docs.text:
For diagnosis, medications, surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, procedures, supplements, or screening changes, include:

  • Not medical advice
  • Discuss with a qualified clinician
  • Possible contraindications, harms, and trade-offs
  • Monitoring considerations
  • Uncertainty level

docs.title: Prohibited automated recommendations
docs.text:
Hermes should not directly recommend that the user:

  • Start, stop, or change treatment or medication
  • Delay urgent medical care or ignore symptoms
  • Choose one treatment option without clinician review
  • Treat screening, diagnostic, or risk-stratification summaries as a diagnosis

docs.title: Preferred framing
docs.text:
Use:

  • “Worth discussing with a clinician”
  • “Evidence suggests, but applicability depends on risk group…”
  • “Potential harms include…”
  • “This changes the evidence map by…”

Avoid:

  • “You should choose…”
  • “This proves…”
  • “This rules out cancer…”

docs.title: Include by default
docs.text:
• New or updated guidelines from NCCN, EAU, AUA, ASCO, NICE, USPSTF, Cancer Australia, or major cancer centers
  • Randomized trials, meta-analyses, and large prospective studies relevant to prostate cancer diagnosis, treatment, surveillance, or outcomes
  • Diagnostic accuracy studies with clear clinical endpoints
  • Patient-reported outcome studies for urinary, sexual, bowel, fatigue, mental health, or survivorship outcomes
  • Safety updates that may affect clinical conversations

docs.title: Exclude or down-rank
docs.text:
• Preclinical-only studies unless clearly practice-relevant
  • Small retrospective single-center studies without a patient-facing implication
  • Press releases without accessible study details
  • Opinion pieces not tied to evidence or guideline changes
  • Commercial claims without independent validation

docs.title: Scoring rubric
docs.text:
Score 0–10. Include items scoring >= 7.5 or items with lower score only if they are important safety/guideline watchlist items.

Dimension Points Evidence quality 0–3 Patient decision relevance 0–2 Recency/novelty 0–1 Applicability to real clinical pathways 0–2 Clarity of risks/limitations 0–1 Source credibility 0–1

docs.title: Required note format
docs.text:
• Citation/title/authors/date/source URL
  • Evidence type and population
  • Category/categories
  • Summary
  • Main finding
  • Patient relevance
  • Limitations
  • Safety/clinical cautions
  • Whether it changes a category page or decision map

docs.title: Patient-Perspective Overview
docs.text: This section turns the evidence library into a patient-oriented decision map.

docs.title: Typical journey
docs.text:
1. Screening or suspicion — PSA, DRE, family history, urinary symptoms, incidental finding
  2. Risk clarification — repeat PSA, PSA density, MRI, risk calculators
  3. Diagnosis — biopsy approach, pathology, Grade Group/Gleason, tumor volume
  4. Staging and aggressiveness — imaging, clinical stage, genomic classifiers if appropriate
  5. Management choice — active surveillance, surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, clinical trial, or palliative focus depending on risk/stage
  6. Follow-up — surveillance, PSA monitoring, side-effect management, recurrence detection

docs.title: Repository rule
docs.text: Summaries should help prepare for a clinician discussion, not substitute for it.

docs.title: Diagnosis and risk
docs.text:
• What is the most likely explanation for my PSA/test result?
  • Should PSA be repeated before further testing?
  • Would mpMRI help decide whether biopsy is needed?
  • If biopsy is recommended, why this approach and what are the risks?
  • If cancer is found, what Grade Group, stage, and risk category is it?

docs.title: Treatment choice
docs.text:
• Is active surveillance reasonable for this risk profile?
  • What are the expected outcomes with surgery vs radiation vs surveillance?
  • What side effects are most likely for someone in my situation?
  • How many similar cases do you manage per year?
  • Should I get a second opinion or multidisciplinary review?

docs.title: Follow-up
docs.text:
• What result would trigger a change in plan?
  • How often would PSA, MRI, biopsy, or imaging be repeated?
  • How will quality-of-life side effects be monitored and treated?

docs.title: A. Before diagnosis
docs.text:
• What prompted testing?
  • What is the PSA trend, PSA density, and age-adjusted context?
  • Is MRI appropriate before biopsy?
  • What are the false-positive, false-negative, and overdiagnosis issues?

docs.title: B. If biopsy is being considered
docs.text:
• Transperineal vs transrectal approach
  • MRI-targeted plus systematic biopsy vs systematic alone
  • Infection, bleeding, urinary retention, and pain risks
  • Whether antibiotics or anesthesia/sedation are relevant

docs.title: C. If cancer is found
docs.text:
• Grade Group/Gleason score
  • Number and percentage of positive cores
  • PSA, PSA density, MRI lesion size, clinical stage
  • Whether genomic classifier or PSMA PET is relevant

docs.title: D. Management options
docs.text:
• Active surveillance
  • Radical prostatectomy
  • External beam radiation therapy
  • Brachytherapy
  • Focal therapy/investigational approaches
  • ADT or systemic therapy for higher-risk/advanced disease

docs.title: E. Outcomes to track
docs.text:
• Cancer control and metastasis-free survival
  • Need for later treatment
  • Urinary incontinence/irritative symptoms
  • Sexual function
  • Bowel symptoms
  • Fatigue, mood, and quality of life

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
• PSA: Prostate-specific antigen, a blood marker that can rise for cancer and non-cancer reasons.
  • PSA density: PSA divided by prostate volume; can help interpret PSA in context.
  • mpMRI: Multiparametric MRI, used to identify suspicious prostate lesions.
  • PI-RADS: MRI scoring system for likelihood of clinically significant cancer.
  • Biopsy: Tissue sampling to diagnose cancer and determine grade.
  • Gleason score / Grade Group: Pathology grading of cancer aggressiveness.
  • Active surveillance: Structured monitoring for lower-risk cancer, with treatment if risk changes.
  • Radical prostatectomy: Surgical removal of the prostate.
  • Radiation therapy: Treatment using targeted radiation, including external beam or brachytherapy.
  • ADT: Androgen deprivation therapy, systemic treatment that lowers androgen signaling.
  • PSMA PET: Imaging test that can detect prostate cancer spread or recurrence in selected contexts.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Which diagnosis-first pathway best reduces unnecessary biopsy while avoiding missed significant cancers?
  • Which patients benefit most from MRI-targeted biopsy, systematic biopsy, or both?
  • How should genomic classifiers change decisions for borderline risk groups?
  • Which active surveillance schedules optimize safety while minimizing anxiety and procedures?
  • Which treatment choices best preserve urinary, sexual, and bowel quality of life for comparable cancer-control outcomes?

docs.title: Latest Updates
docs.text:
Weekly research updates will be listed here in reverse chronological order.

No updates yet.
